topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

My phone buzzed with a notification, and my breath hitched in my throat. A profile picture flashed across the screen: a grinning face I hadn't seen in fifteen years. It was Mark. Mark, the architect of my early-schoolyard misery, the one whose shadow I’d spent years trying to outrun. My fingers froze over the screen, hovering uselessly above the 'Accept' button. A strange, tight knot formed in my stomach, pulling my shoulders up toward my ears. *What in the world?* I reread the name, the familiar, grating sound of it suddenly feeling alien and heavy. I slowly slid the phone face down onto the desk, the silence of my apartment amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

I stared at the notification for a full minute, my jaw slack. It was him. David. The boy who used to dismantle my confidence with a smirk and a well-timed jab. Now, a request sat waiting, a digital ghost from a forgotten era. I felt a sudden, cold prickle run down my spine, making the hairs on my arms stand up. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, shaky exhale. My hands trembled slightly as I navigated to the profile. His latest photo was casual, smiling, completely devoid of the malice I remembered. A slow, bewildered smile began to pull at the corners of my mouth, a feeling so unfamiliar it made my cheeks warm.

[story 3]

The sound of the incoming request was sharp, cutting through the quiet evening. I snatched my phone up, my heart immediately kicking into a frantic rhythm against my ribs. Seeing the familiar username—a name that used to conjure images of playground torment—made my vision swim momentarily. I blinked hard, trying to process the reality of the screen. My lips parted slightly, but no sound came out. I gripped the phone so tightly my knuckles turned white. I dropped it onto the cushion beside me, the sudden absence of the device leaving me feeling oddly adrift. I just sat there, staring at the blank wall, trying to reconcile the small, digital prompt with the vast chasm of memory it represented.
